turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We will hear arguments next in Hudson v. Louisiana. Mr. Burst.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The case that I present to the Court today, I think, is limited in issue and scope, and briefly stated, simply involves where a trial court in Louisiana after hearing the evidence, sustained a motion for a new trial on the basis of insufficient evidence and the defendant was retried. Subse­ quently, before appeal became final, this Court rendered a de­ cision in Burks v. United States. The case was taken to the Louisiana Supreme Court on a writ, first by writ of habeas corpus to the district court, and then by writ of habeas corpus to the Louisiana Supreme Court on certiorari. tiorari on the specific issue. The issue before them was limited, as it is now, precisely and only to the double jeo­ pardy question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Justice Dennis begins his dissent with a citation to Burks, does he not?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: That is correct. And I argued Burks completely. Now, Jackson had not been issued, but Burks was before them.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, we take our State procedure and state procedural remedies as we find them in this Court. And it would appear to me that from Justice Tate's opinion and from the majority opinion, they treat the motion that was made as a 13th-juror type of motion, which Burks left open.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: They do treat it, but they treat it on the same basis as this Court discussed in Jackson v. Virginia. In other words, they applied it on a no evidence versus insuf­ ficient evidence. In fact, in the first page, the first two pages of the opinion of the Supreme Court of Louisiana, they set forth the issue of Burks as rendered by the Chief Justice of this Court and then they set forth the issue before them and they use exactly the same language, only they rule con­ verdict." Then, on page 48, in reference to discussing Burks, he states that this Court decided and reversed on the issue of solely for lack of sufficient evidence to sustain the jury's verdict.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then you would have to disavow Justice Tate's concurring opinion, wouldn't you?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: Yes, I would. I further -- I feel that also in Justice Tate's concurring opinion that he simply states facts that were not mentioned by the trial judge in his ruling. I think the trial judge's ruling is concise and if there is any ambiguity at all in the trial judge's ruling, it would be by virtue of the precise language he used where he said that because can't one read that to mean, either not sufficient evi dence from which a jury could have no reasonable doubt, on the basis of which a jury could find guilt beyond a reasonable doubt, or not sufficient evidence to convince me, the judge, beyond a reasonable doubt?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: I think that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, you could read it either way,

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: I have to just take the words as they're spoken. I was not --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, which way does it mean? Does it mean he doesn't think there's enough evidence to convince him beyond a reasonable doubt, or not enough to have submitted to the jury, and if he means the latter why did he submit it to the jury?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: Well, I have two answers to that ques­ tion. Louisiana laws that existed then did not permit a trial prior to that time -- and I have both sections cited in my judgment notwithstanding verdict at that time?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: In '75. Not at the time of this trial they did not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, at the time of this trial, couldn’t the judge at the end of the trial have said what he said to himself here, and therefore I am entering judgment for the defendant of acquittal notwithstanding the verdict? Could he do that at that time?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: No, sir. The first and only vehicle that a defendant has in Louisiana to raise the issue of insuf­ And that was the law at the time this was tried, that is sub­ sequent --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But Justice Tate had been a member of that Court for many years. Wouldn't you expect him to have known that if that were in fact the case? that’s what he was saying, that's what the law required him to do, and specifically, precisely that. In other words, if at that point in time, if in his opinion there was insufficient evidence, then he was to so hold. And by so holding he has placed the case in a position where it cannot be retried under our Constitution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then you're saying, I take it, that a statute which required a judge to submit this question to the: jury when there was in fact no evidence, would in itself be unconstitutional. Is that your position? that there is no constitutional guarantee of a motion for directed verdict, or even for an appeal for that matter. I feel that as long as all people are treated alike, it makes . no difference. All I'm saying is that in this case, in Louisiana law, there is no motion for directed verdict avail­ able to the defendant in a jury trial and the first time, by dence is on motion for new trial. I don't think that that in case that the ruling of the trial judge could not have been as Hr. Justice Stevens pointed out, as a statement that no rea­ sonable juror could reasonably find guilt on the evidence, don't you?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: Yes, sir. I do. And I feel that-- when I' say the ■ double, I say that, doubly, that what I feel is the faulty premises that the Louisiana court bases its decision on, is' that is one, is that certainly one cannot read the trial judge's statement, his opinion, and it is clear what he is ruling. At the very outside he is saying there was insufficient evidence beyond a reasonable doubt to convict to the State's position. I say he still, even with that finding, that there is no retrial, or should be none.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But he granted a new trial. time, in even the' State I'm from, that was the law. If they -- in effect, what they were saying was, if you move for a new trial, I do waive my right to raise double jeopardy, then I think it's unconstitutional, as requiring him to choose between two constitutional safeguards.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, in a motion for a new trial for error in the admission or rejection of evidence, is there a waiver of any double jeopardy claimed there?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: No, sir, because double jeopardy does, as this Court clearly set out in Burks, that that is not a jeopardy question, where it's an error of law, and where it's and correct that error. But this Court has, I think, clearly said in Burks that where there's a failure to muster suffi­ cient evidence the state doesn't get a second chance. And in . this case is what they got, and indeed they came forth at the second trial with an eyewitness, ten months later. The second position, I think, that is -- again, I will use this.term "faulty premise" that the Louisiana Supreme Court is using, is simply that they are attempting to apply have a dichotomy between no evidence and insufficient evidence that we are not going to apply, in essence, the Burks deci­ sion of the standard of reasonable doubt, as In Re: Winship. I think there, from both ends, that their premise is faulty. And I would assert that as recently as 1980, May of 1980, the court of Louisiana, the Supreme Court of Louisiana, has main­ tained their position, and that is, they have said that in the case of -- if I may have a drink of water. In May of 1980, in the State v. Custard -- it's the state court of Louisiana, they stated then that "This court has yet fully to embrace laid to rest the dichotomy between the no evidence rule and the application of the rule of insufficient evidence beyond a reasonable doubt, and adopted, and indeed said that even on federal, and I recognize that Jackson is a very limited opin­ ion, and it's limited to habeas corpus review by federal dis­ trict judges. to the Supreme Court of Louisiana and advise them that they will have to embrace the reasonable doubt standard.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Burst, what if the trial judge had explicitly relied on Subsection, Subparagraph 5 of Article 851 in granting the new trial? You would not then claim that there was denial of -- ?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: That is accurate; yes, sir. I would not claim double jeopardy. Let me get to my section here and make --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you do disavow the concurring opinion

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: It's totally in the brief. I think the wording of the trial judge could not have been clearer on what he was deciding it under. his statement can be understood to mean that he as a so-called 13th juror was not convinced beyond a reasonable doubt.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: And this is where the trial judge is discussing the motion for a new trial. And about midway down, it begins, "The argument was, however, that there was no evidence presented that would have justified the jury in finding the defendant guilty as charged because there was no evidence beyond a reasonable doubt before the jury of the es­ sential elements to prove the essential elements of the crime.' So he fixed the . issue he was determining it on. His discussion thereafter discusses that basis and his final statement is precisely, again, on it. And I -- perhaps I'm whether he's sitting as a 13th juror or what, at a point in time where a reviewing court, whether it be the trial judge or an appellate court, cites that the evidence was insuffi­ cient beyond a reasonable doubt and at the point in time that becomes final, I believe that the man is acquitted.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if the trial judge didn't do it guilt beyond a reasonable doubt, that is the end of the case as far as double jeopardy, because certainly it is not the position of the Supreme Court of Louisiana.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: At this point, that is accurate. But if this Court were to require the Supreme Court of Louisiana to adhere to the standard of not guilty or of insufficient evidence beyond a reasonable doubt, then it's only by case law that they have interpreted into their cases the standard of no evidence.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: Yes, sir. Well, in Oklahoma -- that might be accurate, I don't know. I know, in Oklahoma, we never1 use the new evidence v. the insufficient evidence split. In other words, it's strictly and always has been whether or not the evidence was sufficient beyond a reasonable doubt. But --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Burst, you did not try this case? Did you try this case?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: No, sir, I did not. In fact, I was not retained until after, or brought into the case until after the appeal. or a basis of a finding that there was insufficient evidence to convince any reasonable triers of fact beyond a reasonable doubt. But I thought -- isn't that inconsistent with your answer to my question when I asked you if the court had, the trial court had explicitly relied on Subparagraph 5?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: Perhaps I am confused with this Subpara­ graph 5 --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That you would not be making this claim? Subparagraph 5 of Article 851.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: That's correct. If he had strictly re­ this Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In other words, one can imagine a state -- I doubt that there is one, but there may be -- which said that anybody convicted of a criminal offense in a trial court is entitled to a new trial as his right, just to entitle them to two bites at the. apple. And so a person is’convicted and he makes

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: None at all.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And wouldn't the same thing be true if the new trial were granted under Subparagraph 5?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: I thought that was the question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, it was. And I thought you answered, yes, it would be.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: In Subparagraph 5, the court made a finding, "It is of the opinion the ends of justice would be better served by granting a new trial.although defendant may be entitled to a new trial as a matter of. . ."

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: "May not be entitled to a new trial."

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: "May not be." Then I would not be before this Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the defendant would hardly use it as a subterfuge.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: That's correct. If the defendant moved on, if he specifically said, I want a new trial on number five, then yes, I totally agree with you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Unless, in fact, there were no evidence in the record of guilt. Say the judge just put it on Section 5, but then the defendant argued, well, he granted it for that reason, but they didn't offer any evidence of guilt at all, and on review of the record you concluded that was right.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: If the trial judge concluded that there was no evidence at all --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He doesn't conclude anything. The trial judge says, I'm going to grant a Section 5 new trial; in the interest of justice, I wasn't persuaded. But supposing a at all, didn't put a single witness on the stand?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: No, that's what I would -- that was my point in answering. If the Rule 5 grant were, in my words, a subterfuge, or actually what the motion was that there was insufficient evidence, then I don't think there could be a new trial.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So the test is what the record shows, not what the judge says?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it's up to this Court to independently

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: No, I didn't say that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what did you say?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You can't have it both ways..

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: No, I don't ask for it both ways. I don't ask; this is not my role.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what do you say? Is it what the judge says or what the record says?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: What I say is, when at some point, when the judge of whatever system it is, in this case the State of Louisiana,when the judge who sits on the issue makes his find­ tice. Is there always permitted to be a new trial? You see, the other side of the coin is, here he used the language you like. There's no evidence. But Justice Tate said, no, but I've looked at the record and there is evidence in the record that was sufficient to go to the jury. Do you agree with Justice Tate when he says that? He doesn't turn on what the judge says. He says there was enough evidence in the record to be submitted to the jury.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: First of all, I would take exception, and on an issue of law.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I understand. Your opinion is-, we just look at what the trial judge says and we don't pay any attention tQ the record.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: It's final.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you conceded that the judge's comment was somewhat ambiguous, did you not?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: Only to the point of whether or not he would have voted the other way, then, that gives rise to a double jeopardy problem?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Just because he disagrees?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: If he is willing to make the ruling un­ der the then law -- as he did in this case, and that is subject to appeal; you know, it couldn't in this case but in future cases it could be -- I realize that Louisiana has a problem right now, if this Court follows my ruling, but that's Louisiana's problem if their structure is not in line with the

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's not what Burks said, is it?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: No, sir. Burks was not a state case. I recognize that. ments since Benton v. Maryland have been the same for pur­ law lies

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But ? .. ' ing only one question, namely, that the evidence does not sup­ port the verdict with evidence beyond a reasonable doubt . Then, what is the test? If there’s a motion for a new trial on that ground, what’s the test that the trial judge should apply? HR. BURST: I think he should apply the test of whe­ ther or not that any trier of fact could find --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could reasonably find?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In other words, that’s Justice Tate's -- it would follow Justice Tate's theory?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: I don't read that into Justice Tate's And he's going behind the record which, I'm told time and time again I can't do, when I come on appeal. And I do take issue with that in the concurrent opinion; yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but his opinion is not inconsistent with the action of the Supreme Court the first time around in refusing to reverse the order granting a new trial, because his theory of the order granting a new trial was that there was enough evidence to go to the jury, but that the interests of justice made it appropriate to grant a new trial. There's nothing inconsistent with his view in affirming the -- in other words, he says the order was right.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: It's Inconsistent in that the standard applied is identical to the standard this Court has laid down in In Re: Winship, whereas the standard where, which the State, of Louisiana is saying was merely insufficient evidence, is identical to the standard that this Court has laid down in In Re: Winship and recognized in Burks and later in Jackson v. Virginia. And, you know, if they can say the same words and say they mean something else, then I would concur with the Court,

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: If you wish to save any time for rebuttal, you have your --

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: I would like to, Your Honor.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Bullers.

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I agree with Mr. Burst that this really is limited and involves interpretation of the particular statutes that The State’s position is that the trial judge, when he made the comment, he said that "I heard the same evidence the jury did." Then he went on to say, "I'm convinced." It does appear somewhat ambiguous, of course. As I said, the State's position is that he was stating his opin­ ion after he had heard the same evidence that the jury heard. Burks and Jackson v. Virginia. The problem that we run into is Of course, the State's position was, we agree with Judge Tate that, although because the trial judge did not have the benefit of Burks and Jackson, he didn't articulate partic­ ularly and he did not assign one particular subsection or another. But even if he had, isn't it really what the record reflects ? But at any rate, the -- in the Supreme Court --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you agree, Mr. Bullers, that at the time of this trial the trial judge had no power whatever to direct a judgment of acquittal? I do. At that time --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even if he did find that there was no evidence whatsoever, the extent of his power was to grant a new trial as a matter of judgment of the court.

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: Yes, sir, that's correct. At that point, that's what it was. That particular provision for appeal. I think the issue comes down to what extent should the trial judge interject himself or be required to interject himself into the trial by jury process? We have a jury of 12 peers that felt the evidence was sufficient was beyond a rea­ sonable doubt. The trial judge differed. So, the only way that I could see logically would be to see what the record really shows. Somebody -- obviously, there's a difference there; that's obvious. But the key is, how -- to what extent should the trial judge interject himself into this process? Should he be required to evaluate the evidence that the jury

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You keep saying the trial judge injected himself in. I thought he sat there by right of the Constitu­ tion of the State of Louisiana. into it? I didn't say he did in this case. I'm just saying this is what it's going to boil down to. You understand as, in other words, in the future --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, I heard you.

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: Right. In the future the procedure that we have now just says that at the conclusion of the trial then you have a right, if he's found guilty, as in this case, then he can take an appeal or ask for a new trial, because the section pertaining to directed verdicts was deleted. I con­ cede that, so that throughout this time was simply to raise new trial. So the issue would be in the future, then, to what extent does the judge have to evaluate the evidence in the jury process, trial by jury process? I'm not saying that he interjected himself in it in this case. He followed the law that since he didn't have the -- since Burks had not been rendered at that time, he didn't

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And he also had a duty in advance of -- he had to submit the case to the jury even if he thought there was insufficient evidence to go to the jury?

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: The way that our Code of Criminal Pro­ cedure, Article 778, was at that time, it would only be by trial judge alone, of course. If there was a jury trial, it would have to be submitted to the jury.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what about at the end of the prosecu­ tor's case, could he have granted directed verdict at that

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: No, he couldn't, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So if he thought there was no evidence of guilt and that there'd been a total failure of proof, he would, time, that was his remedy. We agree to that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there anything at all in the record that supports Judge Tate's view that -- and I know he's an awfully good judge and usually is pretty careful about what he does -- but is there anything in the record to support his view that the judge relied on Section Five?

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: No, Your Honor, the judge didn't state that. Obviously, Judge Tate in his reviewing the entire case felt that's what it was. He obviously differed in the opinion of the judge -- that when the judge said there was no tainly not beyond a reasonable doubt. Judge Tate obviously dis­ agreed with that because he said he felt that the record showed sufficient evidence to give it to the jury. instead of one, and it goes back to, since the trial judge didn't articulate, and even if he had it still seems that

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If your procedure allowed a directed verdict, and the district and the trial judge entered a verdict of not guilty notwithstanding the judgment, would you say that would bar another trial? That would clearly bar another trial, even if there was a --

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: There would be a finding of not guilty and in essence .it would, bar- another trial.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So we just look at the judge's ruling in that case, and we wouldn't really look at the evidence at all?

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: Do you have any other questions?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then the State has no appeal from an order granting a new trial? MR, BULLERS: No, Your Honor, there is no appeal or review by the State, once the judge orders a new trial then we'd have to proceed on with a new trial. There was no higher was probably the most accurate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under Burks or what?

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: Yes, sir. I think that, as I understand. Burks, it would be, is there sufficient evidence to give it to the jury? And I think that Judge Tate considered that, and determined that there was. But of course, he had to look at the record to determine that, obviously. He had to know what the evidence was.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you agree with him?

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: Yes, I do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Shouldn't we remand it, then, for the Supreme Court to apply the right standard and determine whe­ ther there was double jeopardy?

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: That is a possibility. As I under­ stand the majority opinion, what they were saying was that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if you agree with Tate, what else can you do except remand?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In other words, you're saying that the -- are you saying? -- that the Louisiana Supreme Court judg­

turns.name: BULLERS
turns.text: His theory, as I said, appears to be the most accurate. The majority opinion, I think, when they were sorting through Burks and applying it here, got into a He's with the 5th Circuit Court of Appeals now. But if it does come down to the fact that this constitutes double jeo­ pardy, then I think that as you read our statutes, then, Article 851, which sets out the various subdivisions, the judge is going to have to articulate particularly which one and why. And as I said, the trial judge didn't have that bene­ fit at the time. So that's why we're sort of at a loss here. I think that puts us in a difficult position. I think that's true of the appellant as well as the State. Does the Court have any other questions? have anything further, Mr. Burst? You have two minutes remaining.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: They will be very brief, indeed.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: First of all, I think if this Court looks articulated it more precisely if he meant what you say he meant ?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: Well, I think he did. Because he went on to quote --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Supreme Court of Louisiana certainly agreed with you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Supreme Court of Louisiana agreed with you.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: Yes, they did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Agreed with your reading of what the trial judge says.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: The majority did, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Rightly or wrongly.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: But I -- you know, I think he does say that the State of Louisiana is in the same position it was then unless it is reversed in this Court. And that is, that if there is an appeal by the State on a writ, they'll deny it because they're still applying the no evidence test and saying we can't review it unless there's no evidence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or as a matter of state law, the Supreme Court has, never reviews the sufficiency of the evidence, I gather.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's a matter of the state statute, isn't it?

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: That's a matter of -- I think it is statute by interpretation of the Constitution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: T just read it here; yes.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: That is correct. They're saying, what they're saying is, it doesn't arrive_to a legal issue until you can point out that there's no evidence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In other words, the Supreme Court of dence, as a matter of state law.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If they say that's what their Constitu­ tion means, that's what their Constitution means, as far as we're concerned.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We can't say differently.

turns.name: BURST
turns.text: I understand. Thank you very much.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Thank you. The case is submitted.
